does: This paper develops an open-source mechanistic interpretability tool designed to expose and analyze the internal representations learned by AI-based weather prediction models. The tool extracts latent representations from model processors and applies standard analytical techniques including cosine similarity and Principal Component Analysis to identify directions in latent space that correspond to recognizable meteorological features. The authors demonstrate their approach on GraphCast, a graph neural network weather model, presenting preliminary case studies focusing on mid-latitude synoptic-scale weather systems, thereby providing concrete evidence that the tool can isolate interpretable weather-related patterns within these previously opaque neural architectures.
connects: This work builds on the mechanistic interpretability framework that has gained prominence in machine learning for understanding neural network predictions by decomposing them into interpretable components. It directly addresses a critical gap in the emerging field of AI weather prediction, where models like GraphCast have achieved competitive performance with traditional Numerical Weather Prediction systems but remain largely black boxes. The approach extends mechanistic interpretability techniques—historically applied to language models and other domains—to the specialized challenge of weather forecasting, combining classical meteorological knowledge with modern ML interpretability methods to bridge the gap between neural network predictions and domain expertise.
enables: By providing interpretability tools for AI weather models, this work enables meteorologists and forecasters to build justified confidence in neural network predictions for operational use. The framework could facilitate transfer learning and model debugging by revealing which physical processes models capture in their latent representations. Future work could extend this tool to identify failure modes, understand how models handle rare or extreme events, and guide the design of architectures that explicitly encode physical constraints. The interpretability foundation may also enable better integration of AI and traditional NWP approaches and support the development of explainable AI weather systems suitable for high-stakes forecasting decisions and climate applications.
